important_note: As of October 2025, Google retired the Protected Audience API and Topics API for Chrome, and Chrome kept a user-choice model for third-party cookies rather than a forced deprecation. Frame any Privacy Sandbox audit against this current reality, not the earlier deprecation timeline.
worth_it_explanation: Third-party cookie signal loss is ongoing, so the audience and frequency-capping signals a publisher relies on keep eroding even though Chrome did not force a deprecation. This audit is worth the time because it separates the Privacy Sandbox technologies that Google retired from the cookie-independent paths that still apply to Google Ad Manager inventory, and it confirms whether the publisher is using its own first-party signals on the audience it knows best. Both checks read configuration the practitioner can see inside Ad Manager and the Google Publisher Tag, so the findings are concrete rather than speculative.
intro: Privacy Sandbox is a set of browser technologies Google proposed as cookie-independent ways to target, cap, and measure advertising. The status changed in October 2025 when Google retired the Protected Audience and Topics APIs and confirmed Chrome would keep offering users a third-party cookie choice rather than remove the cookies outright. These checks help the auditor frame a publisher's Google Ad Manager setup against continued signal loss, focusing on what was retired, what remains, and the publisher's own first-party identifiers.

audit_checks.title: Privacy Sandbox readiness is not tested
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Third-party cookies are restricted by Safari and Firefox and reduced under Chrome's user-choice model, so a slice of demand already reaches the publisher without a stable cross-site identifier. A publisher that has not checked which Privacy Sandbox technologies still apply to its inventory, and what continued signal loss does to revenue on that slice, is guessing about a real part of its monetization.

educate.base:
Privacy Sandbox is a set of browser technologies Google proposed so advertising could target, cap, and measure without third-party cookies. The set changed in October 2025. Google retired the Protected Audience API and the Topics API for Chrome and Android, citing low adoption, and confirmed Chrome would keep offering users a third-party cookie choice rather than remove the cookies in one step. The interest-based and remarketing-style paths that those two APIs were meant to provide are being phased out, so a publisher cannot plan around them.

Third-party cookies have not gone away, but the signal they carry keeps eroding. Safari blocks them through Intelligent Tracking Prevention, Firefox blocks them through Enhanced Tracking Protection, and Chrome's user-choice model means a share of Chrome traffic also arrives without them. For a publisher monetizing through Google Ad Manager (GAM), that erosion shows up as demand that bids lower, or not at all, when no cross-site identifier is present on the request.

The technologies that remain from Privacy Sandbox are narrower than the original set. Cookies Having Independent Partitioned State (CHIPS), the Federated Credential Management API (FedCM), and Private State Tokens continued past the October 2025 update, while attribution measurement moves toward an interoperable web standard. None of these replaces interest-based targeting, so the durable path for a publisher runs through its own first-party signals rather than a sandbox interest API.

A few signs suggest readiness has not been tested:
  • The audit relies on an older plan that assumed Chrome would deprecate third-party cookies on a fixed date
  • No one has confirmed which Privacy Sandbox technologies still apply to the GAM inventory after the October 2025 retirements
  • The revenue effect of continued signal loss has not been measured on the cookieless slice of traffic
You may want to test readiness by measuring the revenue effect on a slice of traffic that already lacks third-party cookies, for example Safari and Firefox sessions, and reading any GAM reporting that isolates that demand. That measurement grounds the rest of the privacy work in a real number rather than a forecast.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, the cookieless slice of traffic is large enough to read directly, so you may want to compare revenue per thousand impressions on Safari and Firefox sessions against Chrome sessions that still carry cookies. That comparison shows the size of the signal-loss gap before you decide which first-party measures are worth the effort.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, much of the highest-value audience signs in to check out or track orders, so the cookieless gap on that known audience can be closed with first-party identifiers rather than a sandbox interest API. You may want to measure the gap on logged-out, cookieless sessions separately from the signed-in audience so the two get different remedies.

learn_more.source_title: Update on Plans for Privacy Sandbox Technologies

impactful_updates.update_title: Google retires Protected Audience and Topics APIs
impactful_updates.update_summary: Google announced it would retire the Protected Audience API and the Topics API for Chrome and Android, citing low adoption, and confirmed Chrome would keep offering users a third-party cookie choice rather than force a deprecation. CHIPS, FedCM, and Private State Tokens continue, and attribution measurement moves toward an interoperable web standard.

investigate.intro: Confirm which Privacy Sandbox technologies still apply to the Google Ad Manager inventory after the October 2025 retirements, and check whether the revenue effect of continued signal loss has been measured on a cookieless slice of traffic.

privacy_sandbox_status.label: Privacy Sandbox status page
steps: Open the Privacy Sandbox developer post on the October 2025 plans and note which technologies were retired, which continued, and which moved to a web standard.
List the technologies the existing audit plan assumed, and mark any that depend on the retired Protected Audience or Topics APIs as no longer available.
Confirm the plan does not assume Chrome will deprecate third-party cookies on a fixed date since Chrome kept a user-choice model instead.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open Reporting and build or open a report that breaks revenue out by browser, so Safari and Firefox sessions can be read separately from Chrome.
Compare revenue per thousand impressions on the browsers that block third-party cookies against Chrome sessions that still carry them, and record the gap.
Note whether any existing report already isolates demand on cookieless traffic, which shows readiness has been measured rather than assumed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records which Privacy Sandbox technologies the current audit plan assumes, whether each still applies after the October 2025 retirements, and whether the revenue effect of continued signal loss has been measured on a cookieless slice of traffic. A setup where the plan reflects the retired and remaining technologies correctly and the revenue gap on cookieless traffic has been measured passes. A setup that still assumes the retired interest APIs or a fixed third-party cookie deprecation, or that has never measured the cookieless revenue gap, is a fail recorded with the assumption at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which Privacy Sandbox technologies the plan assumes and whether the cookieless revenue gap has been measured above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has not tested its Privacy Sandbox readiness against the current state of the technologies. Google retired the Protected Audience and Topics APIs in October 2025 and Chrome kept a user-choice model for third-party cookies rather than removing them, so the durable path runs through first-party signals rather than the retired interest APIs. The revenue effect of continued signal loss on the cookieless slice of traffic has not been measured, which leaves the privacy plan working from a forecast instead of a number. {{voice_recommend}} measuring revenue per thousand impressions on Safari and Firefox sessions against Chrome sessions that still carry cookies, confirming which Privacy Sandbox technologies still apply to the inventory, and focusing the remaining work on first-party identifiers.

audit_checks.title: Publisher Provided Identifiers are not used for logged-in or known users
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A Publisher Provided Identifier is a first-party signal the publisher controls, and it supports frequency capping and audience features when third-party cookies are not present. A publisher with logged-in or otherwise known users that does not set one gives up a durable, cookie-independent signal on exactly the audience it knows best, so demand on that audience falls back to weaker signals or none.

educate.base:
A Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) is a first-party, publisher-controlled identifier that a publisher sends to Google Ad Manager (GAM) for a user it recognizes. GAM uses it for frequency capping, audience segmentation, sequential ad rotation, and other audience-based controls across devices, and it can carry those signals when third-party cookies or device identifiers are not present on the request. Because the publisher assigns it, the PPID stays with a known user even on browsers that block cross-site cookies.

The value is set client-side through the Google Publisher Tag with setPublisherProvidedId, and Google's requirements keep it non-identifying. The PPID must be hashed or encrypted so it is meaningless to Google, it must not be raw personally identifiable information, and it has to fall within the documented character length. A value that is stable for the same user across sessions is what makes it useful since an identifier that changes on every visit cannot cap frequency or build a segment.

For a publisher with logged-in or otherwise known users, the PPID is the signal that survives third-party cookie loss on its most valuable audience. Without it, requests for a signed-in reader look the same to demand as requests for an anonymous one once the cookie is gone, so frequency caps reset, audience targeting drops out, and the bids that depend on those signals fall back. The known audience is the part of the inventory most able to carry a durable signal, and a missing PPID leaves that value on the table.

A few signs suggest a PPID is missing or weak:
  • The Google Publisher Tag fires for signed-in users without a setPublisherProvidedId call
  • The identifier changes between sessions for the same user, so it cannot cap or segment
  • The value is raw or reversible to personal data rather than hashed or encrypted
You may want to confirm both that a PPID is set for known users and that it is stable and non-identifying. A value that is present but unstable, or stable but identifying, does not meet what the feature needs.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, the known audience is usually the newsletter, registration, or subscription base, so a PPID derived from that account is the signal worth carrying. You may want to confirm the identifier is set the moment a recognized reader loads a page, and that it is the same value each visit so frequency capping and segments hold.
site_type_overlays.saas: On a software-as-a-service property that serves ads to signed-in users, the account identity is already stable, so a hashed PPID built from it is a natural fit. You may want to derive the PPID from a non-reversible hash of the internal account ID rather than from an email or other personal field, so the value stays stable without becoming identifying.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the Google Publisher Tag sets a Publisher Provided Identifier for known users, and whether that value is stable across sessions and non-identifying.

google_publisher_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Sign in as a recognized user, load a page that serves ads, and open the Google Publisher Console with the page's debug parameter.
Inspect the page request details and look for a publisher provided identifier on the ad request. Note whether one is present for the signed-in session.
Reload the page in a new session as the same user and confirm the identifier is the same value, which shows it is stable rather than regenerated each visit.

browser_devtools.label: Browser developer tools
steps: Open the browser developer tools, load a page as a known user, and search the page source or network calls for the setPublisherProvidedId call on the Google Publisher Tag.
Read the value passed and confirm it is hashed or encrypted rather than a raw email, name, or other personal field.
Check the documented character length and format requirements against the value to confirm it is one GAM will accept.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for known or logged-in users, whether the Google Publisher Tag sets a PPID, whether the value is the same across sessions for the same user, and whether it is hashed or encrypted rather than raw personal data. A setup that sets a stable, non-identifying PPID for known users passes. A setup with no PPID on known-user requests, a value that changes between sessions, or a value that is raw or reversible to personal data is a fail recorded with the problem observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a PPID is set for known users and whether it is stable and non-identifying above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not set a Publisher Provided Identifier for its logged-in or known users. A Publisher Provided Identifier is a first-party signal the publisher controls, and it supports frequency capping and audience features when third-party cookies are not present, so it survives the signal loss on the audience the site knows best. Without it, requests for a recognized reader look anonymous to demand once the cookie is gone, and the bids that depend on capping and audience signals fall back. {{voice_recommend}} setting a stable, non-identifying Publisher Provided Identifier through the Google Publisher Tag for known users, deriving it from a hash of the account rather than from raw personal data, and confirming the value is the same for the same user across sessions.
